text: For those patients undergoing ilioanal pouch failure in whom diversion with ileostomy alone is inadequate or inappropriate, pouch excision is required. The contemporary series of ilioanal pouch excisions at St Mark's has been recently published in Diseases of the Colon and Rectum, including a detailed description of the steps involved.
In this video, Professor Sue Clark demonstrates the approach, which has become standardised over time in a high-volume centre.
Ureteric catheters are inserted at the beginning of the procedure, aiding in the identification and protection of the ureters during surgery.
The patient is positioned with a rolled-up towel under the sacrum, covered by a plastic sheet, to ensure good access to the perineum. The patient's arms are wrapped and secured at the sides.
The surgeon carefully enters the abdomen, performing an extensive adhesiolysis to provide sufficient access to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the bowel just above the pouch and divides it using a linear stapler. The blind end of the pouch is held securely with a Babcock clamp.

The surgeon uses a flexible plastic catheter connected to an enema bag to wash out the pouch. Saline mixed with povidone iodine is instilled into the pouch, filling it until visible to the abdominal operator, who then applies pressure to ensure complete drainage. This process is repeated until the effluent runs clear.

The surgeon carefully clears adhesions from the mesentery of the prepouch ileum, identifying the plane between the mesentery and the retroperitoneum. The ureters are visually identified and protected during this process.

The surgeon opens a window between the small bowel mesentery and the retroperitoneum using a surgical clip, passing a tape through the space. Careful inspection and palpation confirm the ureters are positioned behind the tape, while the superior mesenteric artery is in front. Further dissection and traction on the tape lift the mesentery and superior mesenteric vessels away from the retroperitoneum, enhancing surgical access.
The surgeon divides the mesentery between clips, ensuring the division is perpendicular to the bowel to prevent devascularization of the bowel above the pouch.
The surgeon transfixes the superior mesenteric artery pedicle using a proline suture, securing the vessel in place.

The surgeon packs the bowel proximal to the pouch away in the upper abdomen and places retractors to optimize access to the pelvis. The pouch is then carefully dissected free down to the pelvic floor, ensuring clear visualization and separation from surrounding structures.

The surgeon performs dissection in the extrasphincteric plane, using the St Mark's self-retaining retractor to aid deeper access. An energy device is employed to manage the indurated pelvic floor tissue.

The surgeon completes the posterior and lateral dissection, delivering the pouch to facilitate anterior dissection. The pouch and anal canal are carefully examined for integrity, and any residual pouch wall is identified and removed if necessary.

The surgeon closes the subcutaneous tissue in layers using an absorbable suture. An interrupted absorbable suture is used to close the perineal skin, followed by the application of glue for protection. No dressing is applied.
The surgeon removes the ureteric catheters and proceeds to close the abdomen. A suction drain is placed in the pelvis before completing the closure.
We hope this video was informative. Please see the published manuscript for further information on a series of ninety two patients and for further detail on the operative technique.
